attribution.name: Instituto Canoa
attribution.note: Original math activities designed for Brazilian public schools, aligned with BNCC (Base Nacional Comum Curricular). Activities adapted and reframed with Curupira character for the recursive.eco platform.

attribution.name: PlayfulProcess
attribution.note: Grammar construction, Curupira framing, adaptation for recursive.eco.

name: Curupira's Math Forest — Grade 6
description:
Twenty-five group math activities for 6th graders, guided by the Curupira — a forest guardian spirit from Tupi-Guarani mythology with flame-red hair and backwards feet. Based on Instituto Canoa's collaborative math activities designed for Brazilian public schools.

The Curupira doesn't teach math — the Curupira shows children that math already lives in the forest: in the angles of branches, the symmetry of leaves, the probability of rain, and the fractions of shared fruit. Each activity is hands-on, group-based, and designed so that every student contributes.

Instituto Canoa (institutocanoa.org) is a Brazilian non-profit supporting high-quality math education in public schools, in partnership with the Lemann Center at Stanford University.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Brazilian cordel literature woodcuts (xilogravura) — the traditional northeastern Brazilian folk art of hand-carved woodblock prints. The Curupira is a public domain figure from indigenous Tupi-Guarani mythology, first documented by José de Anchieta in 1560.

sections.Activity: Students explore their environment to find and classify angles. Working in groups, they hunt for angles in the classroom, the school yard, and in everyday objects — the corner of a book, the opening of scissors, the hands of a clock. They sketch what they find and sort them into three families: acute (sharp, less than 90°), right (exactly 90°), and obtuse (wide, more than 90°).
sections.Materials: Paper and pencil for sketching. A right-angle guide made from folded paper. Protractors (optional for extension).
sections.How It Works:
1. Fold a sheet of paper in half, then in half again — the corner is your right-angle reference.
2. Walk around the room in pairs. Find 10 angles.
3. For each angle, sketch it and classify: is it smaller than your right-angle guide (acute), the same (right), or bigger (obtuse)?
4. Compare findings with other groups. Did anyone find a straight angle (180°)? A reflex angle (more than 180°)?
sections.Math Concepts: Angle classification (acute, right, obtuse, straight, reflex). Angle as a measure of rotation. Estimation before measurement. Geometric observation in the real world.
sections.Curupira's Hint: When I run through the forest, every turn I make is an angle. A sharp dodge around a tree? That's acute. A lazy bend in the river? Obtuse. The forest is full of angles — you just have to learn to see them.
sections.For Teachers: This activity builds geometric intuition before introducing formal measurement. Let students argue about borderline cases — 'Is that acute or right?' — because the disagreement creates the need for precise tools (protractors). Connects to BNCC EF06MA25.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Recognizing Angles
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students investigate the angles formed by clock hands at different times. They draw clock faces, position the hands, and measure or estimate the angle between them. The key insight: the minute hand sweeps 360° every hour, and the hour hand moves 30° per hour — so every moment creates a unique angle.
sections.Materials: Printed clock faces (blank, with 12 numbers marked). Protractors. Colored pencils for the hour and minute hands.
sections.How It Works:
1. Draw the hands at 3:00. What angle do they form? (90° — right angle)
2. Draw the hands at 6:00. What angle? (180° — straight angle)
3. Now try: at what time do the hands form a 0° angle? (12:00, but also 1:05-ish, 2:10-ish...)
4. Challenge: find a time when the hands form exactly 120°. Can you find more than one?
5. Bonus: at what times do the hands overlap? How many times per day?
sections.Math Concepts: Angle measurement in degrees. 360° as a full rotation. Proportional reasoning (hour hand moves 0.5° per minute). Problem-solving through systematic exploration.
sections.Curupira's Hint: I don't use clocks — I read the angle of sunlight through the canopy. When the sun is low, the shadow-angles are long and sharp. When it's high, the angles flatten. Same math, different clock.
sections.For Teachers: The overlap question (hands meeting ~22 times in 24 hours, not 24) is a classic that surprises students and rewards careful thinking. Use it to introduce the idea that intuition needs checking. BNCC EF06MA25-26.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Angles on the Clock
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Where should you sit in a movie theater to get the best view? Students model a cinema screen and seating arrangement, then calculate the viewing angle from different seats. They discover that the 'best seat' isn't in the front row (too close, extreme angle) or the back row (too far, small angle) — it's somewhere in the middle where the viewing angle is widest.
sections.Materials: Graph paper. Ruler. Protractor. String or yarn (for modeling sight lines).
sections.How It Works:
1. Draw a cinema screen (a horizontal line) at the top of your graph paper.
2. Mark seats at different distances: Row 1 (very close), Row 5, Row 10, Row 15.
3. For each seat, draw lines from the seat to both ends of the screen.
4. Measure the angle between these two lines — this is your viewing angle.
5. Which row gives the biggest viewing angle? Plot your results on a graph.
6. Discussion: Is bigger always better? What about neck angle?
sections.Math Concepts: Applied angle measurement. Inscribed angles. Optimization — finding the 'best' value. Data collection and graphing. Introduction to the idea that geometry solves real problems.
sections.Curupira's Hint: I sit in trees to watch the animals. Too high and everything looks tiny. Too low and the branches block my view. The best watching-spot is always somewhere in between — just like the best seat in a cinema.
sections.For Teachers: This activity connects geometry to optimization — a powerful idea that students will meet again in algebra and calculus. The surprising result (middle rows win) teaches that mathematical investigation can overturn assumptions. BNCC EF06MA25-26.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Cinema Angles
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students explore the concept of 'half' by dividing different shapes, objects, and quantities into two equal parts. The twist: 'equal' doesn't always mean 'identical.' A rectangle can be halved into two rectangles, two triangles, or even two L-shapes — as long as the areas match.
sections.Materials: Sheets of colored paper (squares, rectangles, circles). Scissors. Rulers.
sections.How It Works:
1. Take a square of paper. Fold it in half. How many different ways can you fold it so each half is equal?
2. Now try with scissors: can you cut a square into two equal halves that are NOT rectangles? (Hint: try cutting from corner to corner, or in a zigzag.)
3. Challenge: cut a square into two equal halves where the two pieces are not the same shape. Is this possible?
4. Share your solutions. How do you PROVE two pieces are equal if they look different?
sections.Math Concepts: Fractions as equal parts. The concept of 1/2. Area conservation. Symmetry. Multiple representations of the same fraction. Proof by superposition (overlapping pieces).
sections.Curupira's Hint: In the forest, sharing means everyone gets the same amount, not the same piece. I split a papaya in half — one piece is wide and flat, the other is tall and narrow. Different shapes, same fruit. That's what half means.
sections.For Teachers: This activity challenges the common misconception that 'half' means 'two pieces that look the same.' By producing unconventional halvings, students deepen their understanding of area and equality. BNCC EF06MA07.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — What is Half?
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Using the seven pieces of a tangram, students explore fractions by determining what fraction of the whole square each piece represents. They discover that the two large triangles are each 1/4, the medium triangle is 1/8, and the two small triangles, the square, and the parallelogram are each 1/16 — wait, that doesn't work. The real fractions surprise them.
sections.Materials: Tangram sets (purchased or cut from paper using a template). Grid paper for verification.
sections.How It Works:
1. Assemble your tangram into a complete square. This square = 1 (the whole).
2. Remove the largest triangle. What fraction of the whole is it? How do you know?
3. Use the small triangle as your unit. How many small triangles fit into each piece?
4. Express each piece as a fraction of the whole: large triangle = ?, medium triangle = ?, small triangle = ?, square = ?, parallelogram = ?
5. Add all fractions. Do they sum to 1? If not, find your error.
sections.Math Concepts: Fractions as parts of a whole. Comparing fractions using a common unit. Addition of fractions with unlike denominators. Area relationships. Visual proof.
sections.Curupira's Hint: I know a bird that builds its nest from exactly seven pieces of bark — different sizes, but they always fit together into the same round shape. Nature is a tangram. Every piece matters, and every piece has a fraction.
sections.For Teachers: The tangram fractions are: 2 large triangles (each 4/16 = 1/4), 1 medium triangle (2/16 = 1/8), 2 small triangles (each 1/16), 1 square (2/16 = 1/8), 1 parallelogram (2/16 = 1/8). Total = 4+4+2+1+1+2+2 = 16/16 = 1. BNCC EF06MA07-08.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Fractions with Tangram
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Which is bigger: 3/5 or 2/3? Students develop strategies for comparing fractions WITHOUT finding common denominators first. They use estimation (Is it more or less than half?), benchmarks (0, 1/2, 1), visual models (fraction strips, number lines), and reasoning ('3/5 is 2/5 away from 1, while 2/3 is 1/3 away from 1 — which gap is smaller?').
sections.Materials: Fraction strips (pre-cut or folded from paper). Number lines. Cards with fractions written on them.
sections.How It Works:
1. Draw a number line from 0 to 1. Place these fractions on it: 1/2, 1/4, 3/4, 1/3, 2/3.
2. Now add: 3/8, 5/6, 2/5. Where do they go? Explain your reasoning.
3. Card game: each player draws a fraction card. Who has the bigger fraction? Justify without calculating.
4. Sort a set of 10 fraction cards from smallest to largest using only estimation and reasoning.
5. Discussion: when is finding a common denominator faster? When is estimation better?
sections.Math Concepts: Comparing fractions. Benchmark fractions (0, 1/2, 1). Fraction sense — intuiting relative size. Number line representation. Equivalent fractions.
sections.Curupira's Hint: If I have 3 out of 5 mangoes left and you have 2 out of 3 papayas left — who ate more of their fruit? Don't calculate. Just think: who's closer to finishing?
sections.For Teachers: Fraction comparison without computation builds number sense — arguably more important than procedural fluency. Students who can reason about fraction size catch errors that purely procedural students miss. BNCC EF06MA07-09.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Comparing Fractions
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students analyze bar graphs and pie charts about Brazilian beaches — visitor numbers, water temperatures, distance from cities, pollution levels. They practice reading data, comparing values, and answering questions that require interpreting (not just reading) the graph.
sections.Materials: Printed graphs showing beach data (or projected on screen). Paper for answering questions.
sections.How It Works:
1. Look at the bar graph of beach visitors per month. Which month has the most visitors? Estimate the number.
2. The pie chart shows reasons people go to the beach. What percentage goes for swimming vs. surfing vs. relaxing?
3. Two graphs are shown: visitor numbers and water temperature. What relationship do you see? Does warmer water = more visitors?
4. A graph shows pollution levels at 5 beaches. A tourist wants to swim safely — which beach would you recommend and why?
5. Create your own question about one of the graphs. Trade with another group and answer theirs.
sections.Math Concepts: Reading bar graphs, pie charts, and line graphs. Interpreting data vs. just reading numbers. Correlations between variables. Drawing conclusions from data. Formulating questions.
sections.Curupira's Hint: I track the forest's data too — how many birds return each spring, how the river level changes with rain. Data tells stories. But you have to know which questions to ask, or the data stays silent.
sections.For Teachers: Push students beyond 'reading' graphs toward 'interpreting' — the difference between 'what does the graph say?' and 'what does the graph mean?' The question-creation step (step 5) is the most valuable: it shifts students from consumers to producers of mathematical inquiry. BNCC EF06MA31-32.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Beach Data — Reading Graphs
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students collect and graph data about digital connectivity: screen time, number of apps used, time spent on social media vs. homework. They survey their classmates, organize the results in a table, choose the best type of graph to represent each question, and present their findings.
sections.Materials: Survey sheets. Graph paper. Colored pencils. Rulers.
sections.How It Works:
1. Design a short survey (3-4 questions) about digital habits: How many hours of screen time yesterday? How many different apps did you use? How much time on homework vs. gaming?
2. Collect responses from your group (or the whole class).
3. Organize data in a frequency table.
4. For each question, choose the best graph type (bar, pie, line, pictograph) and explain WHY that type works best.
5. Present your graph and one surprising finding to the class.
sections.Math Concepts: Data collection and organization. Frequency tables. Choosing appropriate graph types. Statistical literacy. Mean and range (informal).
sections.Curupira's Hint: Even the forest has 'connectivity data.' Some trees connect to hundreds of others through underground fungal networks. Others are isolated. If you graphed the forest's connections, what would the chart look like?
sections.For Teachers: The graph-type selection is the key learning moment — students must justify their choice (bar for comparing categories, pie for showing parts of a whole, line for trends over time). Accept creative choices if well-reasoned. BNCC EF06MA31-33.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Connectivity — Digital Data
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students create a statistical 'portrait' of their class by collecting data about themselves: height, shoe size, birth month, favorite subject, number of siblings, distance to school. They organize, graph, and analyze this data to discover patterns in their own community.
sections.Materials: Measuring tapes. Survey sheets. Large poster paper for group graphs. Colored markers.
sections.How It Works:
1. Each group collects data on one variable from the whole class (e.g., Group 1 measures heights, Group 2 surveys favorite subjects).
2. Organize your data: frequency table, ordered list, or tally chart.
3. Create a large poster graph. Label everything clearly.
4. Calculate the mean (if numerical) or the mode (if categorical).
5. Gallery walk: visit each group's poster. What did you learn about your class that you didn't know before?
6. Discuss: if you added a new student, could you predict their data? Why or why not?
sections.Math Concepts: Statistical variables (numerical vs. categorical). Data collection methods. Mean, mode, range. Graphical representation. Statistical inference and prediction.
sections.Curupira's Hint: If I made a portrait of the forest's animals — how tall, how heavy, how many legs — I'd see patterns too. Most are small. Most are insects. The forest's 'class portrait' would surprise you.
sections.For Teachers: The prediction question (step 6) introduces statistical inference — can we use group data to say anything about individuals? This is where math meets philosophy. Let the debate happen. BNCC EF06MA31-34.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Class Portrait — Our Data
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students create bead bracelets using repeating color patterns. By counting the total beads needed for different pattern lengths and bracelet sizes, they discover multiples naturally: a pattern of 3 colors always needs a total that's a multiple of 3. If the bracelet has 24 beads, which patterns fit perfectly?
sections.Materials: Colored beads or paper circles. String or pipe cleaners. Number charts (1-100 grid).
sections.How It Works:
1. Choose a pattern of 2 colors (e.g., red-blue-red-blue). How many beads for a bracelet of length 12? 15? 20? Which lengths work perfectly with no leftover beads?
2. Now try a pattern of 3 colors. Which bracelet lengths give a complete pattern with no gaps?
3. On a 1-100 chart, color all multiples of 2 in one color, multiples of 3 in another. Where do colors overlap?
4. You have exactly 30 beads. List ALL the repeating patterns (2-color, 3-color, 5-color, 6-color...) that fit perfectly.
5. A friend has 28 beads. Can they make a pattern of 3 that fits perfectly? Why or why not?
sections.Math Concepts: Multiples as repeated groups. Divisibility. Common multiples. Factor pairs. Patterns in the multiplication table.
sections.Curupira's Hint: In the forest, patterns repeat everywhere — day-night-day-night is a pattern of 2. The seasons are a pattern of 4. If a vine grows 3 leaves per segment and reaches 21 leaves total, how many segments? That's multiples.
sections.For Teachers: The bracelet context makes multiples tactile and meaningful. When a pattern 'doesn't fit,' students feel the remainder physically — there are leftover beads with no color assigned. This embodied experience of division with remainder is powerful. BNCC EF06MA05-06.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Bracelet Patterns — Multiples
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students play a code-breaking game where secret messages are hidden in a number grid. Only the multiples of a chosen number reveal the letters. Groups take turns creating coded messages using multiples of 2, 3, 4, 5, or 6, then exchanging grids for other groups to decode.
sections.Materials: 10×10 number grids (printed). Letter tiles or stickers. Colored pencils.
sections.How It Works:
1. Choose a secret number (e.g., 4). Write a short word.
2. On your 1-100 grid, place each letter of your word on a multiple of 4 (4, 8, 12, 16...).
3. Fill all other squares with random decoy letters.
4. Give your grid to another group with the hint: 'The key is 4.'
5. They must find all multiples of 4, read the letters in order, and decode the word.
6. Challenge: encode a message using multiples of TWO numbers (e.g., letters on common multiples of 3 and 4 = multiples of 12).
sections.Math Concepts: Identifying multiples. Skip counting. Common multiples (LCM introduction). Pattern recognition. Systematic search strategies.
sections.Curupira's Hint: I leave coded messages in the forest — footprints every 3 meters, broken twigs every 5 trees. Only someone who knows the pattern can follow my trail. Multiples are nature's secret code.
sections.For Teachers: The LCM extension (step 6) happens naturally when students try to use two keys simultaneously. Don't rush to introduce the term — let them discover that 'multiples of both 3 and 4' is a thing before you name it. BNCC EF06MA05-06.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Decoding Multiples
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: A set of open-ended challenges that push students to think deeply about multiples and divisibility: Can a number be a multiple of 6 but not a multiple of 3? Is 0 a multiple of every number? If you know all multiples of 12, do you automatically know all multiples of 6?
sections.Materials: Number charts. Paper for arguments. Mini-whiteboards (optional).
sections.How It Works:
1. True or false (justify!): Every multiple of 6 is also a multiple of 3.
2. True or false: Every multiple of 3 is also a multiple of 6.
3. Find a number that is a multiple of both 4 and 6 but NOT a multiple of 24.
4. What is the smallest number that is a multiple of 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 simultaneously?
5. Discussion: Is 0 a multiple of 7? Debate with your group and prepare an argument.
6. If I tell you N is a multiple of 12, what else do you know about N?
sections.Math Concepts: Divisibility rules. Logical reasoning about multiples. Least Common Multiple (LCM). Factor trees. Mathematical argumentation and proof.
sections.Curupira's Hint: In the forest, every stream is part of a river, and every river is part of a basin. Multiples work the same way — the small ones always fit inside the big ones. If you understand that, you understand everything.
sections.For Teachers: These problems reward mathematical reasoning over calculation. Encourage written justifications. The 0 question is genuinely philosophical — mathematically, 0 = 0 × 7, so yes, but students' intuitions say otherwise. Great debate material. BNCC EF06MA05-06.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Thinking About Multiples
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students develop their own strategies for adding fractions before learning the standard algorithm. Using fraction strips, visual models, and number lines, they figure out WHY you need common denominators and WHAT that means — not just HOW to do it.
sections.Materials: Pre-cut fraction strips (halves, thirds, fourths, sixths, eighths, twelfths). Number lines. Grid paper.
sections.How It Works:
1. Use fraction strips: place 1/3 and 1/4 side by side. How much do they cover together? Is it more or less than 1/2? More or less than 1?
2. Try to find a single fraction strip that matches the combined length. You'll need twelfths!
3. Explain in your own words: why can't you just add 1/3 + 1/4 = 2/7? What goes wrong?
4. Practice: 1/2 + 1/3 = ? Use strips first, then verify with a drawing.
5. Challenge: 2/3 + 3/4 = ? Predict first (more or less than 1?), then compute.
sections.Math Concepts: Addition of fractions with unlike denominators. Common denominators as a shared unit. Estimation of fraction sums. Visual models for fraction operations.
sections.Curupira's Hint: You can't add mangoes and papayas and get mangopayas. You need a common name first — 'fruits.' Fractions are the same: you can't add thirds and fourths until you find a common piece size.
sections.For Teachers: The crucial moment is step 3 — when students articulate WHY 1/3 + 1/4 ≠ 2/7. The strips make the error visible: the '2/7' strip doesn't match the combined length of 1/3 + 1/4. Understanding the error prevents it permanently. BNCC EF06MA10.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Adding Fractions
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students solve fraction problems set in the context of sharing snacks: If a pizza is cut into 8 slices and 3 people eat 2 slices each, what fraction is left? If you have 3/4 of a chocolate bar and give away 1/3 of what you have, how much do you have now?
sections.Materials: Paper circles (pizza models). Fraction bars (chocolate bar models). Worksheets with snack scenarios.
sections.How It Works:
1. A pizza has 8 slices. Maria eats 2/8, Pedro eats 3/8. How much is left? Draw it.
2. You have 3/4 of a cake. You eat 1/3 of what's there. How much cake is left? (Careful — it's 1/3 of 3/4, not 1/3 of the whole!)
3. Three friends share a bag of 24 candies. Ana gets 1/3, Bruno gets 1/4, Clara gets the rest. How many does each person get? Is this fair?
4. Create your own snack-sharing problem. It must involve at least two fraction operations.
5. Exchange problems with another group and solve.
sections.Math Concepts: Fraction operations in context. Fractions of fractions (multiplication preview). Part-of-a-part reasoning. Problem creation as a mathematical skill.
sections.Curupira's Hint: When three monkeys share a bunch of bananas, they don't argue about denominators — they just split fairly. But 'fairly' is harder than it sounds when the pieces aren't equal. That's why you need fractions.
sections.For Teachers: Step 2 is intentionally tricky — '1/3 of what's there' vs '1/3 of the whole' is a critical distinction that previews fraction multiplication. Let students make the error, then discuss. BNCC EF06MA10-11.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Snack Fractions
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students use square grids to visualize fraction operations. By shading portions of a grid, they can see addition, subtraction, and even multiplication of fractions as areas. A 12×12 grid is particularly useful because 12 has many factors (2, 3, 4, 6).
sections.Materials: Printed 12×12 grids (multiple copies per student). Colored pencils. Rulers.
sections.How It Works:
1. On a 12×12 grid (144 squares), shade 1/4 in blue. How many squares? (36)
2. On the same grid, shade 1/3 in red (don't overlap with blue). How many squares? (48)
3. What fraction is shaded total? Count the colored squares and simplify: 84/144 = 7/12.
4. Verify: does 1/4 + 1/3 = 7/12? (Yes! 3/12 + 4/12 = 7/12)
5. Now try 1/4 × 1/3: shade 1/4 of the grid, then shade 1/3 OF THAT shaded area. How many doubly-shaded squares? (12 = 1/12 of the grid)
6. Challenge: show 2/3 - 1/4 on a grid.
sections.Math Concepts: Visual models for fraction arithmetic. Area model of multiplication. Grid-based proof of fraction operations. Equivalent fractions through common gridding.
sections.Curupira's Hint: The forest floor is a grid — patches of sun and shadow. When I say '1/4 of the clearing is in shadow,' you can see it. Math that you can see is math that you understand.
sections.For Teachers: The area model is one of the most powerful representations in mathematics. Students who internalize it rarely make errors like 1/4 + 1/3 = 2/7, because they can 'see' that the answer must be close to 7/12. BNCC EF06MA10-11.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Fraction Squares
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students classify a collection of shapes into groups based on properties THEY choose — not properties the teacher assigns. Different groups will create different classification systems, leading to rich discussion about what makes a useful category.
sections.Materials: A set of 20-30 cut-out shapes (various polygons, including irregular ones). Large paper for sorting. Labels/sticky notes.
sections.How It Works:
1. Spread all shapes on your table. Sort them into groups. You decide the rule.
2. Label each group with your sorting rule (e.g., 'has a right angle,' 'all sides equal,' 'looks like something I've seen').
3. Can any shape belong to more than one group? What does that mean?
4. Now re-sort using a DIFFERENT rule. How many valid classification systems can you find?
5. Gallery walk: visit other groups. Which classification system is 'best'? Can you combine two systems?
sections.Math Concepts: Classification and categorization. Properties of polygons (sides, angles, regularity). Venn diagrams (overlapping categories). Mathematical definitions as tools for sorting.
sections.Curupira's Hint: I sort leaves by shape — round ones, pointed ones, ones with jagged edges. But a leaf can be round AND jagged. Categories overlap in the forest, just like in math.
sections.For Teachers: Resist the urge to provide the 'correct' classification first. Student-generated categories are more meaningful. After the gallery walk, you can introduce standard mathematical terms as names for categories they already discovered. BNCC EF06MA19-20.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Diverse Shapes
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students investigate the family of quadrilaterals — four-sided shapes — and discover the hierarchy: every square is a rectangle, every rectangle is a parallelogram, and every parallelogram is a quadrilateral. But is every quadrilateral a parallelogram? No! Students build the family tree.
sections.Materials: Geoboards or dot paper. Rulers. Protractors. A set of quadrilateral cards (square, rectangle, parallelogram, rhombus, trapezoid, kite, irregular).
sections.How It Works:
1. On a geoboard, make a square. List its properties (4 equal sides, 4 right angles, parallel sides).
2. Stretch the square into a rectangle. Which properties survive? Which are lost?
3. Tilt the rectangle into a parallelogram. Again — what stays, what goes?
4. Keep going: from parallelogram to trapezoid. From trapezoid to irregular quadrilateral.
5. Draw a family tree: which shapes are 'children' of which 'parent' shapes?
6. True or false: 'Every square is a rhombus.' Justify with properties.
sections.Math Concepts: Quadrilateral classification hierarchy. Properties: parallel sides, equal sides, right angles, diagonals. Inclusive definitions (a square IS a rectangle). Logical reasoning.
sections.Curupira's Hint: Trees have families too — the kapok is a cousin of the ceiba, and both are children of the Bombacaceae. Quadrilaterals have the same kind of family tree. Once you see the family, you understand why a square and a rectangle are related.
sections.For Teachers: The hierarchy is genuinely confusing for students. The key insight: definitions should be INCLUSIVE (a square meets all requirements for being a rectangle, so it IS one). Use the family tree to make this visual. BNCC EF06MA19-20.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Quadrilaterals
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students sort triangles by TWO different criteria — sides (equilateral, isosceles, scalene) and angles (acute, right, obtuse) — and discover that these create a 3×3 grid of possible combinations. Do all 9 combinations exist? Can a triangle be both equilateral and obtuse?
sections.Materials: A collection of 15-20 cut-out triangles (varied types). Protractors. Rulers. A large 3×3 grid drawn on poster paper.
sections.How It Works:
1. Measure the sides of each triangle. Sort into: equilateral (3 equal), isosceles (2 equal), scalene (0 equal).
2. Measure the angles. Sort into: acute (all < 90°), right (one = 90°), obtuse (one > 90°).
3. Create a 3×3 grid: columns = side types, rows = angle types. Place each triangle in its cell.
4. Which cells are empty? Can you DRAW a triangle to fill them?
5. Is there a cell that's IMPOSSIBLE? (Yes! Equilateral + right, and equilateral + obtuse.) Why?
sections.Math Concepts: Triangle classification by sides and angles. Cross-classification (two criteria simultaneously). Impossible combinations and why. Sum of angles = 180° (introduction).
sections.Curupira's Hint: Leaves come in triangles too — some sharp and pointy, some wide and flat, some perfectly balanced. But you'll never find a leaf that's equilateral AND obtuse. Nature has rules too.
sections.For Teachers: The 'impossible cells' are the key learning moment: equilateral triangles MUST have all angles equal to 60° (since 180 ÷ 3 = 60), so they're always acute. This introduces the angle sum property without formal proof. BNCC EF06MA19-21.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Grouping Triangles
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: A guessing game: one student describes a polygon using ONLY its properties (number of sides, angle types, parallel sides, symmetry), and others must identify it. The twist — multiple shapes might match the description! Students learn that some property sets define a unique shape, while others leave ambiguity.
sections.Materials: Polygon reference cards. Paper and pencil. A screen or barrier so the describer can't show the shape.
sections.How It Works:
1. Player A picks a shape card (without showing it). They describe it using properties only: 'It has 4 sides. Two pairs of parallel sides. All angles are equal.'
2. Other players guess. If multiple guesses are valid, Player A adds another property until only one shape remains.
3. Record: how many properties did it take? Which shape needed the most clues?
4. Reverse challenge: given a set of properties, list ALL shapes that match.
5. Minimum description: what's the FEWEST properties that uniquely identify a square? A triangle?
sections.Math Concepts: Geometric properties as defining features. Necessary vs. sufficient conditions. Minimal definitions. Logical deduction. Mathematical communication.
sections.Curupira's Hint: 'It has wings and it flies.' Is it a bird? A bat? A butterfly? You need more properties to know for sure. Shapes are the same — you need exactly the right clues. Too few and it's ambiguous. Too many and it's wasteful.
sections.For Teachers: This activity builds the foundational skill of mathematical definition — specifying exactly enough properties to identify something uniquely. It's harder than it seems, and students will be surprised how many clues a square needs. BNCC EF06MA19-21.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — What Property?
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students compare the likelihood of everyday events — from 'impossible' to 'certain' — and place them on a probability line. They discover that probability lives on a spectrum, not in black-and-white categories.
sections.Materials: Event cards (pre-made or created by students). A large number line from 0 to 1 drawn on poster paper. Sticky notes.
sections.How It Works:
1. Sort these events: 'The sun rises tomorrow,' 'A coin lands on heads,' 'It snows in the Amazon,' 'You roll a 7 on a standard die,' 'Someone in class has a birthday this month.'
2. Place each event on the probability line from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain).
3. Debate: where exactly does 'It rains tomorrow' go? What information would help you decide?
4. Create 5 events of your own: one impossible, one certain, one 50/50, and two 'in between.'
5. Two events have the same probability — how could you tell? What makes probabilities equal?
sections.Math Concepts: Probability as a scale from 0 to 1. Certain, impossible, likely, unlikely events. Comparing probabilities. The role of information in estimating probability.
sections.Curupira's Hint: Will the jaguar appear on the trail today? I can't be certain, but I know the signs — fresh tracks mean 'likely,' old tracks mean 'unlikely,' no tracks mean 'maybe never.' Probability is how you read the signs.
sections.For Teachers: Resist correcting 'wrong' placements immediately — let students argue about where events belong. The disagreement IS the learning. Note: some events (weather, birthdays) require empirical data, while others (coins, dice) can be calculated. This distinction is important. BNCC EF06MA28-30.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — More Likely!
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students design and test games to determine if they're fair. They play games with dice, coins, and spinners, collect data, and compare observed results to theoretical predictions. A game is 'fair' when all players have an equal chance of winning — but how do you prove it?
sections.Materials: Dice (6-sided). Coins. Spinners (homemade or commercial). Tally sheets. Calculators.
sections.How It Works:
1. Game 1: Roll two dice. Player A scores if the sum is even, Player B scores if it's odd. Play 30 rounds. Is this fair?
2. Game 2: Roll two dice. Player A scores if the product is even, Player B scores if it's odd. Play 30 rounds. Fair?
3. For each game: list ALL possible outcomes. Count favorable outcomes for each player.
4. Compare your data (what happened) to the theory (what should happen). Are they close?
5. Design a game that LOOKS fair but secretly favors one player. Can your classmates detect the trick?
sections.Math Concepts: Experimental vs. theoretical probability. Sample space. Equally likely outcomes. Fairness as equal probability. Law of large numbers (more trials = closer to theory).
sections.Curupira's Hint: A game is only fair if every player has the same chance. When the forest animals play, the jaguar always wins the racing game — but the sloth always wins the patience game. Choose the right game for the right fairness.
sections.For Teachers: Game 2 is sneakily unfair: even products outnumber odd products 27 to 9 (because any even factor makes the product even). Students will be surprised. This is a beautiful entry point to sample space analysis. BNCC EF06MA28-30.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Fair Game
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students work with mystery bags containing colored objects. Without looking inside, they draw objects one at a time (replacing each time), record results, and try to figure out the bag's contents. How many draws do you need before you can confidently guess the composition?
sections.Materials: Opaque bags. Colored cubes, balls, or marbles (5-10 per bag). Tally sheets. Fraction/percentage charts.
sections.How It Works:
1. Teacher prepares bags with known contents (e.g., 3 red, 2 blue, 1 green). Students don't know the contents.
2. Draw one object, record its color, put it back. Repeat 20 times.
3. Based on your data, guess: how many of each color are in the bag?
4. Repeat for 20 MORE draws (40 total). Does your guess change?
5. Open the bag and check! How close was your prediction?
6. Discussion: why does more data help? Could 20 draws ever give a completely wrong answer?
sections.Math Concepts: Probability estimation from data. Law of large numbers. Relative frequency. Prediction and inference. Sampling with replacement.
sections.Curupira's Hint: I've never seen the inside of an anthill, but I've watched ants come out for years. By counting red ants vs. black ants vs. leaf-cutters, I can guess what the colony looks like inside. That's statistics — seeing the invisible from the visible.
sections.For Teachers: This is one of the most engaging probability activities. Let students be surprised when 20 draws give misleading results — and then see how 40 draws are more reliable. This builds intuition for sample size. BNCC EF06MA28-30.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Probability Bag
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students sort a collection of 3D shapes (prisms, pyramids, cylinders, cones, spheres) using criteria they choose. They count faces, edges, and vertices, and discover Euler's formula: F + V - E = 2 for any convex polyhedron.
sections.Materials: 3D shape models (plastic or built from cardboard nets). Table for recording face/edge/vertex counts.
sections.How It Works:
1. Sort your 3D shapes into groups. What rule did you use? (Shapes that roll vs. shapes that don't? Shapes with flat faces vs. curved?)
2. For each polyhedron (flat-faced solid), count: Faces (F), Vertices (V), Edges (E). Record in a table.
3. Look at your table. Can you find a pattern? Try F + V, or F - E, or F + V - E.
4. Does your pattern work for ALL your polyhedra? Test it on one you haven't counted yet.
5. Does the pattern work for a cylinder? Why or why not?
sections.Math Concepts: 3D shape classification. Faces, edges, vertices. Euler's formula (V + F - E = 2). Polyhedra vs. non-polyhedra. Pattern discovery in geometry.
sections.Curupira's Hint: Stones in the river are smooth (no edges, no vertices). Crystals in the cave are sharp (many edges, many vertices). The forest has every shape — you just need to learn how to count their pieces.
sections.For Teachers: Euler's formula is one of mathematics' most beautiful results — and students can discover it themselves from data! When they find F + V - E always equals 2, let them be amazed before explaining why. Note: it fails for shapes with holes (torus). BNCC EF06MA22-23.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Grouping 3D Shapes
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students explore how 3D shapes cast 2D shadows. A cylinder can cast a circle or a rectangle depending on its orientation. A cone can cast a circle or a triangle. Students predict, then test with a flashlight, discovering that a single 3D shape can produce multiple different 2D shadows.
sections.Materials: 3D shape models. Flashlight or lamp. White paper or wall (projection screen). Drawing paper.
sections.How It Works:
1. Hold a cube in front of the light. What shadow does it cast? Sketch it.
2. Rotate the cube. How many different shadow shapes can you create? (Square, rectangle, hexagon!)
3. Predict: what shadow does a cylinder cast from the side? From the top? Test it.
4. Challenge: which 3D shape can cast a circular shadow, a square shadow, AND a rectangular shadow? (A cylinder!)
5. Reverse challenge: I show you a triangular shadow. Which 3D shapes could have made it? List as many as you can.
sections.Math Concepts: 3D to 2D projection. Cross-sections of solids. Multiple representations of the same object. Spatial reasoning. Reversibility of geometric thinking.
sections.Curupira's Hint: At sunset, my shadow stretches long and thin — you might think I'm a snake! Shadows lie. They show you one piece of the truth, not the whole shape. A good mathematician looks at the shadow and imagines what's casting it.
sections.For Teachers: The hexagonal shadow of a cube (when held corner-down) is genuinely surprising and memorable. This activity develops spatial visualization — one of the strongest predictors of STEM success. BNCC EF06MA22-23.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Geometric Shadows
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.Activity: Students investigate nets — the flat patterns that fold up into 3D shapes. How many different nets can a cube have? (Answer: 11.) Students systematically find them all, then design nets for other polyhedra and test if they fold correctly.
sections.Materials: Graph paper. Scissors. Tape. Cardboard squares (6 per student for cube nets).
sections.How It Works:
1. Start with 6 squares. Arrange them so they fold into a cube. Tape and test.
2. Find a DIFFERENT arrangement that also folds into a cube. How many can you find?
3. Some arrangements of 6 squares DON'T fold into a cube. Why not? What goes wrong?
4. Systematic search: there are exactly 11 cube nets. Can your group find them all?
5. Extension: design a net for a triangular prism. For a tetrahedron. For an octahedron.
6. Challenge: given a net, can you predict which face will be opposite which before folding?
sections.Math Concepts: Nets as 2D representations of 3D shapes. Spatial visualization. Systematic enumeration. Geometric properties (opposite faces). Problem-solving through physical models.
sections.Curupira's Hint: A butterfly starts flat inside its chrysalis and unfolds into something three-dimensional. A net is the same — a flat creature waiting to become a shape. You just have to know where to fold.
sections.For Teachers: Finding all 11 cube nets is a genuine combinatorial challenge. Don't tell students there are 11 — let them search and develop their own counting strategies. Rotations and reflections of the same net count as one net. BNCC EF06MA22-24.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Investigating Nets
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.About: Three activities that take angles from abstract measurement to lived experience. Students begin by finding angles in their environment, then explore them in clocks (where angles move and change) and cinema seating (where the best angle is a real optimization problem). The progression moves from observation → measurement → application.
sections.Curupira Says: Angles are how the forest bends. Every branch fork, every river curve, every bird flight-path is an angle. Once you learn to see them, you'll never look at a forest trail the same way.
sections.For Teachers: This trio covers BNCC EF06MA25-26. Start with 'Recognizing Angles' for a tactile introduction, move to 'Clock Angles' for proportional reasoning, and finish with 'Cinema Angles' for applied optimization. Each activity builds on the vocabulary and intuition of the previous one.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Angles — Measuring the Forest
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.About: Fractions as the math of sharing. 'What is Half?' challenges the assumption that equal means identical. 'Tangram' uses a puzzle to discover exact fraction values. 'Comparing Fractions' builds fraction sense — the ability to reason about size without computing. Together, they build conceptual understanding before any algorithm.
sections.Curupira Says: The forest shares everything — rain, sunlight, soil, fruit. But sharing isn't just splitting in two. It's knowing what 1/4 of the sunlight means for a seedling, or what 2/3 of the rain goes to. Fractions are the language of sharing.
sections.For Teachers: These three activities build fraction sense before procedural fluency. By the end, students should be able to reason about fraction size intuitively, not just compute. This conceptual foundation prevents errors like 1/3 > 1/2 ('because 3 > 2'). BNCC EF06MA07-09.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Fractions — Sharing the Harvest
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.About: A progression from reading graphs (Beach Data) to collecting and creating graphs (Connectivity) to using data for self-understanding (Class Portrait). Students move from consumers to producers of statistical information, ending with data about themselves — making statistics personal.
sections.Curupira Says: I keep data about the forest in my head — which trees grew this year, which streams dried up, where the animals moved. You keep your data in graphs. Same idea, different format. The question is the same: what is the data telling you?
sections.For Teachers: The graph-type selection moments (bar vs. pie vs. line) are critical — they teach that representation choices matter. A pie chart that shows trends over time is technically accurate but misleading. Push students to justify their choices. BNCC EF06MA31-34.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Graphs — Reading the Forest's Data
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.About: From bracelets to codes to deep reasoning. Students discover multiples through repeating bead patterns, apply them in a code-breaking game, then wrestle with tricky logical questions about divisibility. The progression: tactile → playful → abstract.
sections.Curupira Says: Patterns in the forest repeat: seasons cycle, tides rise and fall, fireflies flash in rhythm. Every pattern has a number behind it — a multiple, a frequency, a beat. Mathematics is just the forest's rhythm, written down.
sections.For Teachers: The LCM concept emerges naturally when students try to use two pattern lengths simultaneously (in bracelets) or two code keys (in decoding). Don't introduce LCM formally until students have felt the need for it. BNCC EF06MA05-06.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Multiples — Patterns in Nature
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.About: Three approaches to fraction operations: strips (1D), snack scenarios (context), and grids (2D area). Each representation teaches something different — strips show WHY common denominators are needed, snack problems show WHERE fractions appear in life, and grids show HOW multiplication works visually. Together: a complete toolkit.
sections.Curupira Says: When the animals share a feast, they don't argue about denominators. But they DO argue about fairness. Learning fraction operations means learning to share fairly — with numbers as your proof.
sections.For Teachers: Use all three representations. Students who only learn one method often struggle to transfer. The strips work best for addition, the grids work best for multiplication, and the snack problems connect everything to meaning. BNCC EF06MA10-11.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Fraction Operations — Dividing the Bounty
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

items.name: Polygons — Shapes of Leaves and Stones

sections.About: Four activities that build geometric thinking from the ground up. Students start by creating their own classification systems (Diverse Shapes), then explore two important families in depth (Quadrilaterals, Triangles), and finish with a game that tests whether they can COMMUNICATE geometric properties precisely (What Property?).
sections.Curupira Says: Every leaf is a polygon if you squint. The star-shaped ones, the diamond-shaped ones, the ones that look like arrows — the forest designed them all. But to protect the forest, I need to name the shapes precisely. That's what geometry gives you: precise names for beautiful things.
sections.For Teachers: This is the richest topic unit in Grade 6, with four activities instead of three. The progression from free classification → family hierarchies → property-based identification builds both vocabulary and reasoning. BNCC EF06MA19-21.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Polygons — Shapes of Leaves and Stones
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

items.name: Probability — What the Forest Might Do

sections.About: A probability trilogy: from intuitive likelihood judgments (More Likely!) to rigorous fairness analysis (Fair Game) to inference from data (Probability Bag). Students learn that probability is not just about dice — it's about making good decisions with incomplete information.
sections.Curupira Says: I can't be certain the rain will come tomorrow. But I read the clouds, I feel the wind, I watch the ants. With enough signs, uncertainty shrinks. That's probability: shrinking uncertainty by paying attention.
sections.For Teachers: The key progression: qualitative probability (More Likely) → quantitative probability with known outcomes (Fair Game) → probability estimation from unknown outcomes (Probability Bag). This mirrors the historical development of probability theory itself. BNCC EF06MA28-30.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Probability — What the Forest Might Do
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

items.name: Solids — The Forest in Three Dimensions

sections.About: Three perspectives on 3D shapes: classification (Grouping), projection (Shadows), and unfolding (Nets). Each activity connects the 3D world to its 2D representations — and back. Students who can move fluently between dimensions develop powerful spatial reasoning.
sections.Curupira Says: The forest IS three dimensions. Trees go up. Roots go down. Rivers go sideways. But when you draw a map of the forest, you flatten it into two. That's what shadows and nets do — they flatten reality so your mind can hold it.
sections.For Teachers: Spatial reasoning is one of the strongest predictors of STEM success, and it's trainable. These three activities attack it from different angles: counting structure (faces/edges/vertices), imagining projection (shadows), and mentally folding/unfolding (nets). BNCC EF06MA22-24.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Solids — The Forest in Three Dimensions
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.About: Nine activities spanning fractions, multiples, and fraction operations. Together they build number sense — the ability to reason about quantity, proportion, and relationship without defaulting to rote procedures. A student with strong number sense can estimate, check their work, and recognize when an answer doesn't make sense.
sections.The Thread: Every activity in this domain starts with meaning (What does this number represent?) before moving to procedure (How do I calculate?). This is the Instituto Canoa philosophy: understanding first, algorithm second.
sections.For Teachers: Number sense develops through varied experiences — manipulatives, visual models, contextual problems, open-ended reasoning. No single activity builds it; the cumulative effect of all nine does. Allocate roughly 40% of the year's math instruction to this domain.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Numbers & Operations — The Forest's Arithmetic
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

items.name: Geometry & Measurement — Shapes in the Canopy

sections.About: Ten activities covering angles, polygons, and solids. The progression: 1D measurement (angles), 2D classification (polygons), 3D reasoning (solids). Students develop the vocabulary, visual skills, and logical reasoning that geometry demands — while staying connected to the physical world through hands-on investigation.
sections.The Thread: Geometry is the most visual branch of mathematics, and these activities exploit that: folding paper, casting shadows, building nets, sorting shapes. Students who struggle with symbolic math often excel here because they can see and touch the ideas.
sections.For Teachers: Don't rush past the classification activities — they build the logical thinking (definitions, hierarchies, necessary/sufficient conditions) that students need for proof-based geometry in later years. BNCC EF06MA19-26.

illustrations.scene: Curupira forest spirit — Geometry & Measurement — Shapes in the Canopy
illustrations.license: AI Generated, Curupira is public domain folklore

sections.About: Six activities spanning data representation and probability. In a world of information overload, these skills are survival tools: reading graphs critically, collecting data honestly, recognizing what probability can and cannot tell you. Students learn that data always tells a story — but you have to check who's telling it.
sections.The Thread: Statistics and probability are the most immediately relevant math topics for citizenship. Every news article, every health claim, every election poll uses these ideas. Students who understand them are harder to fool.
sections.For Teachers: This is often the shortest unit in the year, but it shouldn't be. Allocate at least 25% of the year — these are the math skills students will use most as adults. The graph-creation and probability-bag activities are the most engaging and should anchor the unit. BNCC EF06MA28-34.
